title: podman machine init can silently downgrade the amount of memory
body:
### Issue Description

If too much memory (unclear what makes it too much) is requested during `podman machine init` via `--memory`, a machine is created with less memory than requested (how the amount of memory is determined is also unclear, it's not the same as the default). No warning or error is provided to indicate the amount of memory in the machine is less than requested.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman machine reset --force`
2. `podman machine init --memory $(echo "$(sysctl -n hw.memsize) / 1048576" | bc) --now`
3. `podman machine ssh free`


### Describe the results you received

On my laptop with 64GiB of RAM, about 3GiB of RAM is allocated to the podman machine.
```
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:         3028992      499524     2436788         828      248504     2529468
Swap:              0           0           0
```

### Describe the results you expected

On my laptop with 64GiB of RAM, I expected to see 64GiB of total RAM allocated to the podman machine and reported by free.

### podman info output

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

### Additional information

I have confirmed that it is possible to allocate much more RAM to a podman machine, for example, on my laptop, I can create a podman machine with 63GiB of RAM.
```
podman machine reset --force && podman machine init --memory 64512 --now && podman machine ssh free
...
Machine "podman-machine-default" started successfully
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:        64801776      966592    64164680         824      248292    63835184
Swap:              0           0           0
```

comments.body: I just upgraded to [v5.4.2](https://github.com/containers/podman/releases/tag/v5.4.2) and this is still an issue. (Not trying to poke at this, just confirming that yes, latest was tried now that there's a new latest.)

@Salynn thanks for the report.  I also noticed the unit is wrong on the error message while trying to reproduce your issue.  Could you post `~/.config/containers/podman/machine/<provider>/podman-machine-default.json` ?

I'm going to guess the mystery is largely determined by the hypervisor.  I tested on Linux and asked for 61000 memory.  Linux shows 58G.  I also confirmed the process was told 61000

```
baude      29550 16.1  3.2 67740292 2098452 pts/0 Sl  20:16   0:37 /usr/bin/qemu-system-x86_64 -accel kvm -cpu host -M memory-backend=mem -drive if=virtio,file=/home/baude/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/qemu/podman-machine-default-amd64.qcow2 -object memory-backend-memfd,id=mem,size=61000M,share=on -m 61000 -smp 8 -fw_cfg name=opt/com.coreos/config,file=/home/baude/.config/containers/podman/machine/qemu/podman-machine-default.ign -qmp unix:/run/user/1000/podman/qmp_podman-machine-default.sock,server=on,wait=off -netdev stream,id=vlan,server=off,addr.type=unix,addr.path=/run/user/1000/podman/podman-machine-default-gvproxy.sock -device virtio-net-pci,netdev=vlan,mac=5a:94:ef:e4:0c:ee -device virtio-serial -chardev socket,path=/run/user/1000/podman/podman-machine-default.sock,server=on,wait=off,id=apodman-machine-default_ready -device virtserialport,chardev=apodman-machine-default_ready,name=org.fedoraproject.port.0 -pidfile /run/user/1000/podman/podman-machine-default_vm.pid -chardev socket,id=virtiofschar0,path=/run/user/1000/podman/virtiofschar0 -device vhost-user-fs-pci,queue-size=1024,chardev=virtiofschar0,tag=decc9669be081e525bc1374616c259d1d69d -display none
```

I agree documentation should probably be updated on this.  When I submit a PR to fix the unit issue, I'll add something in there about when you approach the maximum memory your system has, the hypervisor/provider may make the choice to assign less memory than was requested.

@baude 
> I also noticed the unit is wrong on the error message while trying to reproduce your issue.

Not sure what you mean? What error message? Looks like you're possibly using `qemu` as your hypervisor while I'm using `applehv`? Maybe `qemu` provides an error and `applehv` doesn't?

> Could you post ~/.config/containers/podman/machine/<provider>/podman-machine-default.json ?

Sure thing, contents of ~/.config/containers/podman/machine/applehv/podman-machine-default.json
```json
{"Created":"2025-04-08T18:58:13.193049-07:00","GvProxy":{"Debug":false,"MTU":0,"LogFile":"","PidFile":"","SSHPort":0},"HostUser":{"Rootful":false,"UID":501,"HostUserModified":false},"LastUp":"0001-01-01T00:00:00Z","Mounts":[{"OriginalInput":"","ReadOnly":false,"Source":"/Users/zeffron/Documents/Janet/mediawiki","Tag":"85779d815d6d29432a22b315c12b1e4bb9f6","Target":"/mediawiki","Type":"virtiofs","VSockNumber":null}],"Name":"podman-machine-default","Resources":{"CPUs":16,"DiskSize":100,"Memory":65536,"USBs":[]},"SSH":{"IdentityPath":"/Users/zeffron/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/machine","Port":53279,"RemoteUsername":"core"},"Version":1,"ImagePath":{"Path":"/Users/zeffron/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/podman-machine-default-amd64.raw"},"AppleHypervisor":{"Vfkit":{"LogLevel":"panic","Endpoint":"http://localhost:53309","BinaryPath":null,"VirtualMachine":{"vcpus":16,"memoryBytes":68719476736,"bootloader":{"kind":"efiBootloader","efiVariableStorePath":"/Users/zeffron/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/efi-bl-podman-machine-default","createVariableStore":true}},"Rosetta":false}},"Starting":false,"Rosetta":false,"Ansible":null}
```

> I'm going to guess the mystery is largely determined by the hypervisor.

Possibly, see below?

>  I tested on Linux and asked for 61000 memory. Linux shows 58G. I also confirmed the process was told 61000

Not sure exactly what you're looking at when you say "Linux shows 58G". If it's the output of `free`, be aware that free doesn't show the actual "physical" memory available to the system, but the memory available to processes, so it's always going to be a bit less. The best method I've found to get the "physical" memory available to the podman machine VM (only tested for the applehv hypervisor) is `sudo dmidecode --type memory | grep Size | awk '{ sub(/B$/, "i", $3); print $2$3; }' | numfmt --from auto --to-unit Mi | awk '{ sum += $1 } END { print sum }'` which gives the result in what should be the same format as provided to `podman machine init --memory`.

Performing that check for the problematic VM from my reproduction case yields:

```
core@localhost:~$ sudo dmidecode --type memory | grep Size | awk '{ sub(/B$/, "i", $3); print $2$3; }' | numfmt --from auto --to-unit Mi | awk '{ sum += $1 } END { print sum }'
65536
```

So the VM can see that it's been given 64GiB of memory. But somehow the OS inside the VM is only seeing ~3GiB? Could somehow be a hypervisor problem but possibly a vfkit issue? Possibly somehow related to the virtio balloon used to request memory back from the VM?

No, the error bug I was referring to is if you try to add more than the system reports as RAM:

```
❯ podman machine init --memory 65000
Error: requested amount of memory (65000 MB) greater than total system memory (65965854720 MB)
```
@cfergeau any comment here specifically with vfkit ?

> [@cfergeau](https://github.com/cfergeau) any comment here specifically with vfkit ?

I just tried the steps in the initial report on my M1 with podman 5.3.2, and I get the expected amount of memory:
```
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:        65703612      873772    65117280         836      264216    64829840
Swap:              0           0           0
```
The virtualization framework errors out if you ask for too much memory, and  there should not be virtio-balloon devices by default, so I don’t think this is what is causing this issue.
No idea what can be going on.

> I just tried the steps in the initial report on my M1 with podman 5.3.2

I just downgraded to podman 5.3.2 and tried again to see if maybe it was a regression and got the same behavior. So whatever it is, it seems to impact (based on these two data points) Intel macs only.

> > I just tried the steps in the initial report on my M1 with podman 5.3.2
> 
> I just downgraded to podman 5.3.2 and tried again to see if maybe it was a regression and got the same behavior. So whatever it is, it seems to impact (based on these two data points) Intel macs only.

I missed that this is on an Intel mac, I can test it tomorrow.

No such issue on an intel mac either:
```
$ podman machine ssh free
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:        32853816      667456    32327680         808      240852    32186360
Swap:              0           0           0
```

I tested on:
```
$ system_profiler SPHardwareDataType
Hardware:

    Hardware Overview:

      Model Name: MacBook Pro
      Model Identifier: MacBookPro15,1
      Processor Name: 6-Core Intel Core i7
      Processor Speed: 2,2 GHz
      Number of Processors: 1
      Total Number of Cores: 6
      L2 Cache (per Core): 256 KB
      L3 Cache: 9 MB
      Hyper-Threading Technology: Enabled
      Memory: 32 GB
      System Firmware Version: 2069.80.3.0.0 (iBridge: 22.16.13051.0.0,0)
      OS Loader Version: 582~3311

$ sw_vers 
ProductName:		macOS
ProductVersion:		15.3.1
BuildVersion:		24D70

$ podman -v
podman version 5.3.2
```

I’ll try upgrading  macOS in case there’s a regression.
podman/vfkit/… are installed with the installer from podman.io, not through brew.

> I’ll try upgrading macOS in case there’s a regression.

Also  works fine on Intel+macOS 15.4
Can you try to give about 32GB to the VM in case your issue is caused by a too high memory value? On my machine, 32768 is used:
```
$ echo "$(sysctl -n hw.memsize) / 1048576" | bc
32768
```

> Can you try to give about 32GB to the VM in case your issue is caused by a too high memory value?

32 GiB works on my machine (it's what I'm using in the meantime). I've been able to go as high as 63GiB without issue. I'll see if I can the exact memory value that breaks things. I'll also try uninstalling from brew and seeing if installing from the installer package makes a difference somehow).

> > Can you try to give about 32GB to the VM in case your issue is caused by a too high memory value?
> 
> 32 GiB works on my machine (it's what I'm using in the meantime). I've been able to go as high as 63GiB without issue. I'll see if I can the exact memory value that breaks things.

The exact memory value where things break is 63GiB + 1 B. 63GiB is the last size that works as expected.

> I'll also try uninstalling from brew and seeing if installing from the installer package makes a difference somehow).

I tried this, and it still has issues.

> The exact memory value where things break is 63GiB + 1 B. 63GiB is the last size that works as expected.

I don’t have intel hardware with that much memory :-/ Could you paste the vfkit commandline (`ps aux |grep vfkit`)? I don’t think there will be anything special in there as you say that `dmidecode` inside the guest shows all the memory.

Apple Virtualization Framework has a [`maximumAllowedMemorySize` property](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/virtualization/vzvirtualmachineconfiguration/maximumallowedmemorysize?language=objc) which vfkit does not use, but I expect an error to be returned when using too much memory.

> Could you paste the vfkit commandline (`ps aux |grep vfkit`)? I don’t think there will be anything special in there as you say that `dmidecode` inside the guest shows all the memory.

`/usr/local/Cellar/podman/5.4.2/libexec/podman/vfkit --cpus 8 --memory 65536 --bootloader efi,variable-store=/Users/zeffron/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/efi-bl-podman-machine-default,create --device virtio-blk,path=/Users/zeffron/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/podman-machine-default-amd64.raw --device virtio-rng --device virtio-vsock,port=1025,socketURL=/var/folders/h0/j6c1j6ms36n8g62fsvpt5djm0000gn/T/podman/podman-machine-default.sock,listen --device virtio-serial,logFilePath=/var/folders/h0/j6c1j6ms36n8g62fsvpt5djm0000gn/T/podman/podman-machine-default.log --device virtio-net,unixSocketPath=/var/folders/h0/j6c1j6ms36n8g62fsvpt5djm0000gn/T/podman/podman-machine-default-gvproxy.sock,mac=5a:94:ef:e4:0c:ee --device virtio-fs,sharedDir=/Users,mountTag=a2a0ee2c717462feb1de2f5afd59de5fd2d8 --device virtio-fs,sharedDir=/private,mountTag=71708eb255bc230cd7c91dd26f7667a7b938 --device virtio-fs,sharedDir=/var/folders,mountTag=a0bb3a2c8b0b02ba5958b0576f0d6530e104 --restful-uri tcp://localhost:53551 --device virtio-vsock,port=1024,socketURL=/Users/zeffron/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/podman-machine-default-ignition.sock,listen`

> Apple Virtualization Framework has a [`maximumAllowedMemorySize` property](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/virtualization/vzvirtualmachineconfiguration/maximumallowedmemorysize?language=objc) which vfkit does not use, but I expect an error to be returned when using too much memory.

I took the [Running Linux in a Virtual Machine sample code](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/virtualization/running-linux-in-a-virtual-machine?language=swift) from the Apple Virtualization Framework documentation.

First I modified it to output the `maximumAllowedMemorySize`, for which it output 68719476736 (64GiB exactly).

Then I unmodified it, grabbed the kernel and initramfs for Fedora 41 (following the instructions from the documentation) and saw that the kernel did see 64GiB on boot: `Memory: 65667396K/67108472K available (20480K kernel code, 4340K rwdata, 16032K rodata, 4776K init, 5104K bss, 1432456K reserved, 0K cma-reserved)`.

So Apple Virtualization Framework seems capable of creating a VM with the full 64GiB, and Fedora (which I believe is what podman uses as its base OS for its images) seems capable of working with that.

One thing I did notice is that in the 3GiB podman machine, the kernel is claiming a 128MiB memory block size, whereas in the 64GiB sample VM the kernel claims a memory block size of 1024MiB. In the 63GiB podman machine, the kernel claims the memory block size is 2048MiB. No idea if the block size is an actual lead or immaterial.

> (which I believe is what podman uses as its base OS for its images) 

podman uses fedora coreos, which should be close enough to fedora.
If you want to dig further, you could build [vfkit](https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/tree/main/contrib/scripts) from git, and start VMs with [these scripts](https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/tree/main/contrib/scripts) so that you can check the output of `free`

fedora coreos images can be found here https://fedoraproject.org/coreos/download?stream=stable and they use ignition (-> use `start-with-ignition.sh`). You need the « Apple Hypervisor » image.

You can compare with fedora cloud images from https://fedoraproject.org/cloud/download , these ones use cloud-init  (-> use `start-with-cloud-init.sh`). You need a « raw » image.

Is it possible to add here the exact number that 63Gib+1B is? 
This are the numbers for 48Gb:

```
% echo "$(sysctl -n hw.memsize)"               
51539607552
% echo "$(sysctl -n hw.memsize) / 1048576" | bc
49152
% system_profiler SPHardwareDataType  | grep Memory         
      Memory: 48 GB
```

> Is it possible to add here the exact number that 63Gib+1B is? This are the numbers for 48Gb:

Correction, I was actually only able to get to an accuracy if 63GiB + 1 MiB, because podman specifies memory in MiBs. That number is 64513.

63 GiB exactly is 6451, and 64GiB is 65536.

So:

```
48Gib*1024 = 49152MiB
49152*1024 = 50331648KiB
50331648 * 1024 = 51539607552 bytes
```

hmm, without exact knowledge of internal implementation:

unsigned int16 max value is 65535

```
64*1024 = 65536 MiB
65536*1024 = 67108864 KiB
67108864 * 1024 =  68719476736 bytes

63*1024 = 64512 MiB
(64512 + 1) *1024 = 66061312 KiB
66061312 *1024 = 67646783488 bytes
```

Trying here (with 48Gb) I get same message:

```
Error: requested amount of memory (64513 MB) greater than total system memory (51539607552 MB)
```

Can you request 128GiB (131072Mib) to see at least what message you get when requested memory is more than available one?

> Can you request 128GiB (131072Mib) to see at least what message you get when requested memory is more than available one?

@albfan, If I request more memory than the system has (be it 1 MiB more or 128GiB) then I get the error message about requesting too much memory.


> podman uses fedora coreos, which should be close enough to fedora. If you want to dig further, you could build [vfkit](https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/tree/main/contrib/scripts) from git, and start VMs with [these scripts](https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/tree/main/contrib/scripts) so that you can check the output of `free`
> 
> fedora coreos images can be found here https://fedoraproject.org/coreos/download?stream=stable and they use ignition (-> use `start-with-ignition.sh`). You need the « Apple Hypervisor » image.
> 
> You can compare with fedora cloud images from https://fedoraproject.org/cloud/download , these ones use cloud-init (-> use `start-with-cloud-init.sh`). You need a « raw » image.

@cfergeau that's an excellent idea. I am trying to do so, but the coreos image will not boot completely, and the Cloud image seems to not set up an account, so I have not been able to see what the memory post-boot looks like yet. I will keep trying.

> [@cfergeau](https://github.com/cfergeau) that's an excellent idea. I am trying to do so, but the coreos image will not boot completely, and the Cloud image seems to not set up an account, so I have not been able to see what the memory post-boot looks like yet. I will keep trying.

I tried the Fedora 42 cloud image, and that booted and let me in fine. It also exhibited the 3GiB instead of 64GiB issue. So this seems to not be a podman issue but a lower layer for sure. Should I open an issue with vfkit?

> Should I open an issue with vfkit?

Yes please so that we can track it in the right place, however this looks like a virtualization framework bug.
please cross post the new issue so we can keep track of things and get this closed assuming resolution.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
